segments.title: Fleet organization terminology overview
segments.summary: The episode is introduced as a concise explanation of fleet organization titles and what different groupings mean.
segments.body: The opening makes the episode a reference point for fleet, squadron, flotilla, and related naval organization terms. It frames the subject as a vocabulary problem that affects how readers understand naval force structure, not just as a list of labels.

evidence.note: Opening states the video explains fleet organization and begins defining fleet as a whole navy or constituent part.

segments.title: Fleet as whole navy or geographic/mission command
segments.summary: The opening defines fleet as either a whole navy or a major geographic or mission command.
segments.body: The opening establishes that fleet can mean either an entire navy or a major geographic or mission command. Dr. Clarke uses the term as a scale marker, showing how naval organization can refer to national force structure as well as operational groupings. That definition sets up the later distinctions between squadrons, flotillas, task forces, and task groups.

evidence.note: The opening defines fleet as either a whole navy or a major geographic or mission command.

segments.title: Squadrons as primary-combatant groupings
segments.summary: The squadron section explains how primary combatants can be grouped administratively or operationally.
segments.body: The squadron section narrows the scale from fleet-level organization to groups of primary combatants. Dr. Clarke explains that squadrons can be administrative or operational, depending on context and navy. This moment is useful because it keeps squadron distinct from flotilla instead of treating both as generic collections of ships.

evidence.note: The squadron section explains how primary combatants can be grouped administratively or operationally.

segments.title: Flotillas as small-fleet groupings
segments.summary: The flotilla section explains the small-fleet meaning and its connection to destroyers and torpedo craft.
segments.body: The flotilla section defines the term as a small-fleet grouping with a strong connection to destroyers and torpedo craft. Dr. Clarke uses it to explain why lighter forces often receive their own organizational language rather than being folded into capital-ship structures. The distinction matters for reading older fleet orders and modern references to grouped escorts.

evidence.note: The flotilla section explains the small-fleet meaning and its connection to destroyers and torpedo craft.

segments.title: Division depends on squadron or flotilla size
segments.summary: The division section explains how divisions sit inside squadrons or flotillas and vary with formation size.
segments.body: The division section places the term between larger squadrons or flotillas and the individual ships inside them. Dr. Clarke explains that the exact meaning varies with formation size and naval practice, so the label should be read in context. That helps readers avoid treating division, squadron, and flotilla as interchangeable hierarchy words.

evidence.note: The division section explains how divisions sit inside squadrons or flotillas and vary with formation size.

segments.title: Task force and task group distinctions
segments.summary: The task-force section uses Falklands and amphibious examples to distinguish task forces and task groups.
segments.body: The task-force section moves from administrative labels to formations assembled for a mission. Dr. Clarke uses Falklands and amphibious examples to distinguish a larger task force from the task groups operating inside it. The segment is the most operational part of the explainer because it shows labels changing with mission, command, and self-sufficiency.

evidence.note: The task-force section uses Falklands and amphibious examples to distinguish task forces and task groups.

segments.title: Task groups as self-sufficient components of a task force
segments.summary: The conclusion explains task groups as self-sufficient pieces that make up larger task forces.
segments.body: The conclusion defines task groups as self-sufficient pieces within a larger task force. Dr. Clarke uses that scaling to show how naval formations can be broken into capable subgroups without losing the larger command structure. The point ties the terminology together by linking labels to practical command and sustainment.

evidence.note: The conclusion explains task groups as self-sufficient pieces that make up larger task forces.
